summary:
Liu Bang, Emperor Gaozu of Han (reigned 202–195 BC), was a peasant-born rebel leader who defeated the aristocratic Xiang Yu in the [[event:1124|Chu-Han Contention]] to become founder of the [[event:77|Han Dynasty]] — one of the most consequential dynasties in Chinese history — demonstrating that political ability and popular legitimacy could trump birth and military genius.

Liu Bang's background was exceptional for a dynasty founder: he was a village official of no particular distinction before the [[event:1123|Fall of the Qin Dynasty]] gave him the opportunity to rise. His success stemmed from political astuteness, willingness to delegate to talented subordinates (including the strategist Zhang Liang and administrator Xiao He), and a governing philosophy of lighter taxation and reduced state interference after Qin's oppressive rule. As emperor, Gaozu maintained the Qin administrative system (commanderies, prefectures) but relaxed the harshest laws, granted amnesty to former enemies, and allowed the economy to recover. He faced the immediate crisis of the powerful [[event:1133|Xiongnu Empire]] and the [[event:1230|Seven Kingdoms Rebellion]] potential from his own enfeoffed relatives — problems he managed through compromise rather than confrontation, establishing patterns his successors followed.
